items.title: How to afford a mountain bike? (Explained)
items.summary: Mountain bikes vary greatly in quality, design, purpose, and price. If you’re on a budget and looking for the cheapest…
items.content_html:
Mountain bikes vary greatly in quality, design, purpose, and price.

If you’re on a budget and looking for the cheapest bike possible, or if you’re wondering if building a bike is as cheap as it sounds (it’s not), or if you want to know if it’s possible to pay for a mountain bike in installments, or if you want to know what kind of pricing to expect on the cheaper end of the downhill racing bike scale, you’ve come to the right place.

How Can You Afford a Mountain Bike?

The answer to this question greatly depends on what level of quality you want your bike to be and how much disposable income you have.

Mountain bikes cost anywhere from $400 to $10,000, so before we give a definitive answer, we should narrow down what you’re looking for and what your financial constraints are. A basic bike costs $400 to $800, a mid-tier bike costs $1000 to $2500, and a truly great bike costs $3000 to $10,000.

The first place to look when your budget is severely limited is the online resale market.

It’s here that you’re most likely to find a deal from someone who just wants to get rid of an old bike and doesn’t mind if they break even or lose money on the purchase.

You can bid on online auctions or even haggle with the seller if you’re willing to be crafty.

Here’s some good news if you’re on a budget: the absolute cheapest new $400 hardtail (this means that only the front wheel has a suspension system) mountain bikes are of respectable quality.

Companies like Trek and Specialized make beginner bikes that use good materials and are assembled to be sturdy and reliable, and these bikes can even do some more advanced single-track riding if you’re willing to put up with their limitations.

If $400 is still too much for you to spare all at once, consider using a credit card, finding a bike store that offers a layaway plan, or taking advantage of a bike-specific credit union loan.

If you have a steady income but can’t afford to drop $1000 to $2000 on a new bike, the above options still apply—credit cards, layaway plans, and other loans are all possible. At this level, you can expect to pay $100 to $300 per month for several months and afford a great mid-level piece of equipment with a full suspension.

How Can You Afford a New Mountain Bike?

Some bike stores offer layaway plans, and you can contact them to ask about this option. Basic credit cards typically have a credit limit of around $500 to $1000, and this range would cover a sold low-to-mid-tier mountain bike if you want to pay for it over several months.

A credit card with a larger, let’s say, $5000 credit limit could net you a similar deal for a higher-end bike if you’re willing to make monthly payments for a longer time. Again, credit unions are known for offering bike loans, which is a great option that only suffers from the limitation that you must already be a member of a credit union to take out a loan.

You can strategize your purchase by simply looking at your monthly income and picking an amount that you’re able to subtract from it while still meeting your other costs.

Always make sure that you take note of the interest rate on the loan—if you’re lucky, you might be able to find a payment plan with an interest-free option if you pay off the loan within a certain time frame. Also, always make sure that you factor in the interest you’ll be paying and any annual credit card fees before you go ahead with the loan.

Can You Finance Mountain Bikes?

You can finance a mountain bike like you would any other large purchase—with a credit card, a loan, or a layaway arrangement with a store. Mountain bikes can cost thousands of dollars, so it’s no surprise that you’d want to pay for them with a loan.

What is a Good Price to Pay for a Mountain Bike?

There are three different tiers of mountain bike: hardtails, full-suspension bikes, and downhill racers. Expect to pay $400 to $800 for a hardtail, somewhere between $1000 and $2500 for a full-suspension bike, and between $3000 and $10,000 for a downhill racer.

Is Building a Mountain Bike Cheaper than Buying One?

You might think that building your bike would be cheaper than buying it because you’re doing the work of assembling the thing, but this is not the case.

Bike manufacturers have business arrangements with parts makers that allow them to buy parts in bulk, so they’ll always be able to undercut a lone individual.

However, if you’re quite savvy and you play your cards right, you might be able to construct your bike more cheaply by taking advantage of deals on online markets or using spare parts. This option does have the advantage of full customization if you’re willing to put in the work and learn how to build a bike.

Final Thoughts

The price you pay for your mountain bike comes down to how much you’re willing to spend. There’s a large price range, and if you’re willing to take out a loan and pay it down for a long time, you can afford even the higher-end bikes while on a budget.

items.title: Whats wrong with trek bikes (Explained)
items.summary: In the cycling community, you’ll sometimes hear people—particularly serious riders with high-end bikes—talk down to others about using Trek bikes.
items.content_html:
In the cycling community, you’ll sometimes hear people—particularly serious riders with high-end bikes—talk down to others about using Trek bikes.

But is there really anything wrong with Trek? Since Trek makes Bikes that span the whole gamut of bike quality and specialization, and they’re one of the world’s largest bike manufacturers, their brand gets associated in people’s minds with entry-level bikes and entry-level bikers.

In truth, Trek has nothing to be ashamed of about its products, which are well-built and retain their value for years. Some people find reasons to disagree with the values Trek associates with as a brand, like its relationship with the police or Lance Armstrong.

What’s Wrong with Trek Bikes?

Trek bikes are reliable and well-built—there are many good reasons they’re one of the world’s most popular and successful bike brands. If you’re feeling uncertain about buying a Trek bike, you shouldn’t. They’re a safe, middle-of-the-road option for whatever type of bike you’re after.

Every company has its foibles, though.

The most frequently reported mechanical problem with Treks has to do with pedal cleat systems for road bikes, which can get worn out easily. Also, the company received negative customer service reviews overall, according to one survey on Trustpilot.

Despite these foibles, Trek doesn’t deserve an across-the-board reputation for delivering a bad product or handling customer interactions poorly. Most anti-Trek sentiment has to do with the company’s status as a basic, standard, or even bland option in some bikers’ minds, or the company’s public relations stances.

Are Trek Bikes Good Quality?

Trek bikes have a deserved reputation for quality.

One reason for this is the high-quality frameset that Trek builds its bikes around.

Trek is known for making light, strong, and visually appealing frames with light metal alloys like carbon fiber or aluminum and assembling them using a special process called “invisible welding” that minimizes welding seams, often with a cool color scheme to boot.

Trek also approaches the difficult problem of frame geometry in a generalized way.

The geometry of a bike frame refers to its shape and how that shape affects your body’s positioning on the bike.

In some cases, riders must adjust the geometry of the bike themselves by selecting the correct wheel length, but Trek offers the same model of bike in different layouts.

Smaller cyclists might like a certain bike but prefer it with geometry that’s suited to them, and Trek offers that, along with frames suited to aggressive, speedy riders and relaxed, slow riders.

Common Problems with Trek Bikes

Trek is such a ubiquitous brand that asking about its common problems is like asking about the common problems with bikes in general.

Nonetheless, Trek bikes have had a major recall recently in 2021 because of widespread issues with Bontrager pedals falling off due to a technical design oversight.

This is more of a one-time problem than a deep flaw in Trek’s designs, but bikers should still hold Trek accountable for the incident, which caused some minor injuries.

Trek has a business partnership with police forces in the US, where it supplies some departments with bikes. The police came under intense scrutiny in the US in 2020, which could have brought Trek some negative publicity. Also, Trek has a PR relationship with Lance Armstrong, the world-famous cyclist who lost his Olympic and Tour de France medals in a doping case.

Do Trek Bikes Keep Their Value?

Higher-end Trek bikes are a good investment. You shouldn’t expect an entry-level Trek to be worth that much to someone else years later when they could easily afford a new one, but the higher-end Treks hold their value remarkably well. Higher-end Trek designs have stood the test of time, and some cyclists even use or refurbish Treks from decades ago.

Are Old Trek Bikes Good?

Old Trek designs have proven to be reliable and desirable, with some cyclists using frames from as far back as the 1980s. There are whole websites devoted to cataloging vintage Trek bikes since some cyclists prefer the steel frames that were popular earlier in the history of bike manufacturing.

Final Thoughts

If you hear someone at the bike shop or out on the trail disparaging Trek bikes, know that it’s probably because of some other reason than the actual quality of the bikes. People may have political disagreements or personal dislikes of the company, or they might feel like Treks are too basic of an option for them, but don’t let that give you the wrong impression of Trek.

items.title: Should I Upgrade a Trek 820 Mountain Bike?
items.summary: The Trek 820 is ideal for newer riders who want something that’s reliable, inexpensive, and will get them on the…
items.content_html:
The Trek 820 is ideal for newer riders who want something that’s reliable, inexpensive, and will get them on the trail. If you’re looking to add some comfort and ease of use to your trek 820 as an entry-level rider, by all means, you can upgrade things like the handlebar grips, the saddle, or the pedals. But if you’re an experienced single-track rider looking for something that can tear up the dirt with ease, you’re better off saving for a higher-end bike than upgrading an 820.

What Upgrades Can You Add to a Trek 820?

In theory, you can change any aspect of an 820 you please—you can change out the derailleur, get a new suspension fork, or even switch out the 820’s rim-based brakes for disc brakes if you want to. In practice, it makes a lot more sense to get a bike that’s already designed with the purpose you want it for in mind. This means that it makes more sense to stick to more superficial, comfort-focused upgrades on a bike like the 820, like a new saddle, pedals, or handlebar grip, because the 820 is an entry-level bike.

Should You Upgrade a Trek Mountain Bike?

It doesn’t make sense to upgrade the major components on the Trek 820, but some minor features do make sense to improve. Above all, it doesn’t make sense to pour a ton of time and money into outfitting a basic bike with the best features when you can simply get a bike that already has them and is designed to support them. But if you have a Trek 820 that you’ll be putting through lots of use and you’re willing to put in some extra money to make it as comfortable as can be, minor upgrades like pedals, grips, and saddle are a good idea.

How Can You Upgrade a Trek 820 Mountain Bike?

To replace a component on your Trek 820, you can either take the bike to a shop and have a mechanic perform the replacement, or you can perform the replacement yourself. If you’re set on replacing a component like the drivetrain, the derailleur, or the braking system, it’s best to let a professional handle the operation. A lot of fine-tuning goes into setting up components like these, and a tiny problem can make the whole mechanism stop functioning. On the other hand, upgrades like tire replacements, saddle, pedal, or grip replacements are easy to learn and are worth doing yourself.

What Is the First Thing to Upgrade on a Mountain Bike?

This question has many different answers or no answer at all—the most important thing to upgrade depends totally on the rider’s physical needs and what kind of riding he or she wants to do. Get a feel for your bike, take it for a ride or two, and then decide what feels off. Is the saddle too rigid? Get a more comfortable one. Do your arms feel cramped and pushed together? Get a wider pair of handlebars. The most important thing to upgrade is whatever part of the bike gets in the way of you having the best ride you can.

Is it Worth Upgrading an Entry Level Bike?

It’s not worth giving your entry-level mountain bike a complete overhaul and turning it into a trail-gobbling machine. This would take significantly more time, effort, and money than simply getting a bike that already has a superior drivetrain, derailleur, braking system, front suspension fork, and so on. Imagine installing each of those components, or at least paying someone to do it, when you could just get a premade bike. On the flip side, a fancy bike with nice components isn’t worth getting if all you’re planning to do is some light riding on fire roads, and a bike like the Trek 820 may be a serious bargain for you.

Final Thoughts

The Trek 820 is in no way a bad or subpar bike, it’s just a basic option that tries to be both inexpensive and reliable—and succeeds. Its price is quite low if you take its overall quality into account, and if you’re looking for the best bang for your buck, the 820 is it.

The 820 is an old-school build, and serious trail riders back in the day would use similar steel-frame designs to do difficult technical descents, so it can in fact shred trails if you’re willing to work with its limitations. If you want to give it an overhaul, go ahead, but understand that the 820 is designed for low-impact trail riding, not downhill racing.

items.title: Trek Roscoe 7 Alternatives (Explained)
items.summary: As winter turns to spring and Mountain Biking seas son is right around the corner, many people are looking to…
items.content_html:
As winter turns to spring and Mountain Biking seas son is right around the corner, many people are looking to get into the sport and purchase their first bike.

The Trek Roscoe 7 has become a popular ride for many beginners and experienced riders alike- so let’s take a look at what the Trek Roscoe 7 has to offer. 

Trek Roscoe 7

The Trek Roscoe 7 is a mid-fat trial hardtail with 27.5+ wheels and plus-sized tires, a plush suspension fork, and a wide range drivetrain with gears to spear.

The aluminum frame has good protection for a hardtail.

The Trek Roscoe 7 has a parallel seat, 65-degree head tube angle, space for 2 water bottles, semi-internal cabling and comes in multiple sizes so each rider can find one that fits them perfectly. 

Is the trek Roscoe 7 a cross country bike?

And while this is technically a cross country bike, the Trek Roscoe 7 is designed for more aggressive trial riding 

So, what does that mean for the new riders? This makes the Trek Roscoe 7 stable for beginners to get their footing in the sport and experiment with both cross country and trial riding. 

For the more experienced rider, this bike has the capability to handle the more extreme trails and the chain stay lengths is short of 430 mm, which delivers a playful handling. The breaks are the one aspect that could use some upgrading; however, beginner riders won’t find this as much of an issue. 

Trek Roscoe 7 Alternatives 

How does the Trek Roscoe 7 stand up to some competitors? Depending on your budget or experience level, there are a few contenders out there:

Vitus Neculas 29 VRS- 

The Vitus Neculas 29 VRS is built from lightweight and strong aluminum, has a threaded bottom bracket and externally routed gear and brake cables for easy maintenance. Although no dropper post, there’s a cable port on the top of the down tube if you want to add one later, which allows this bike to grow with the rider. 

With a stiff frame and good tires, this bike is better suited for surface trails compared to natural terrain. 

Cannondale Trail SE 4 

The Cannondale Trail SE4, however, has a frame and feature components that allows this bike designed for low-impact trials, gravel roads, and leisure rides to adapt to more intense trials. 

The selling point of this bike is its adaptability to future upgrades: the tapered head tube makes the fame compatible with almost all modern forks, and has both front and rear boots axles that give the rider plenty of flexibility to upgrade down the road; has a Boost axle spacing and a 1x drivetrain and dropper post compatibility; if you upgrade the fork, you wont need to change the front wheel to match. 

Merida Big Trail 500

The Merida may not be the first bike to come to mind when looking for a hardtail, but its capability and details make it a good option for those with a tight budget. 

The aluminum frame with double-butted tubes and real axle spacing on 29in wheels has a tapered-steerer fork allowing for upgrades.

However, the geometry stands out most on this bike- unlike modern bikes that are moving towards longer, more stable geometry, the Merida has a shorter frame that varies in thickness- something to keep in mind when shopping. 

Spealised Fuse 27.5 (2021)

This bike is a real competitor - The frame is compatible with 29er hoops, too, but has a 10mm longer head tube than the Fuse 29 and a bottom bracket (BB) height optimized for the smaller wheels.

Features include a Boost rear axle, threaded BB and two bottle mounts. Cables are routed internally from the head tube to BB.

The former is compatible with a tapered-steerer fork, adding more upgradability.

This is a premium-feeing bike that is best suited for trial centers. And for all that, the price point is not much higher than the Trek Roscoe 7.  

Final Thoughts 

After looking at some competitors, there are not many beginner bikes in the same range that out-preform the Trek Roscoe 7.

While the brakes and dropper posts are the bikes biggest flaws, those can be upgraded over time.

Furthermore, the Roscoe's new geometry should keep it relevant for a while. This bike can grow with you as a rider as you transition from beginner to more experienced mountain biker.

And the adaptability means this bike can handle trails as well as cross country. 

Unless you are looking for a bike in a different price range or a more advanced bike, you cannot go wrong with the Trek Rosco 7.

One last comment for the beginners looking to purchase a new bike, whether it be the Trek Rosco 7 or an alternative: the pandemic is still having an affect on the supply chain for bikes. This is resulting in a shortage and months of backorders for mountain bikes all over the country. When ordering your bike, be prepared to wait months for it to become available.

items.title: 7 Things To Know About A Trek Mountain Bike's Weight Limit
items.summary: Mountain biking is a fun way to get outdoors and stay fit and healthy. However, if you’re a bit larger…
items.content_html:
Mountain biking is a fun way to get outdoors and stay fit and healthy.

However, if you’re a bit larger in size, or if you want to attach a lot of cargo to your mountain bike, it’s important to make sure that your mountain bike can support the weight—you don’t want to damage the bike.

Most mountain bikes, including Trek bikes, have a weight limit of 300 pounds. If you’re excited to find the bike that’s right for your needs, read on!

 

Trek mountain bike weight limit

As a rule of thumb, a Trek mountain bike can safely support up to 300 pounds. This is true of most other brands of mountain bike as well. While there are plenty of factors that can change the maximum weight that a bike can hold without breaking, such as the material the frame is made from, the number of spokes, and the speed that you ride the bike, bike technicians agree that 300 pounds is a good general weight limit for mountain bikes.

It’s also worth mentioning that the figure of 300 pounds is more of a guideline than a hard and fast rule.

Safety guidelines are meant to be as cautious as possible to minimize the chance of danger, so in practice, a bike might be able to support more than 300 pounds at some risk to the rider.

There are also too many factors in play to come up with one weight limit that suits every bike.

Trek 3700 mountain bike weight limit

The Trek 3700 has an aluminum frame, which means that it’s slightly more likely to have problems when carrying a lot of weight.

Aluminum bike frames are designed to be both durable and conveniently lightweight, but when it comes to carrying heavy loads, they don’t provide as low of a center of weight for the rider.

This means that it might be harder for a heavy rider to maintain control of a Trek 3700 when going down hills at higher speeds.

However, the Trek 3700 does have the advantage of an open, comfortable design which is easy for people of all sizes to mount and dismount, and it’s cheaper than a steel frame bike like the Trek 820.

If you do plan on riding the Trek 3700 with a heavy load, it might be a good idea to invest in stronger wheels, since they’re typically the first part of a bike to show signs of stress.

Trek 820 mountain bike weight limit

The Trek 820 has a steel frame, so it’s a bit easier to control under heavy weight. Steel frames are considerably heavier than aluminum ones, so they provide a lower center of weight and more stability when the bike has a heavy load on it.

If you plan on loading up your mountain bike with lots of cargo for a trip, or you’re on the heavier side, the Trek 820 will provide plenty of stability and security for you.

How much weight can a Trek mountain bike hold?

A Trek bike can hold up to 300 pounds. While there are many factors that can affect this figure, if you stick to this guideline, you’ll avoid any major damage to your bike.

A Trek bike can likely exceed this limit for short periods of time without showing signs of trouble, but it would be risky to yourself and the bike to consistently ride while exceeding the weight limit.

Can a 400-pound person ride a bike?

Yes, a 400 pound person can ride a bike! Unfortunately, Trek does not make bikes with strong enough frames or wheels to accommodate 400 pounds.

But this does not mean at all that a 400-pound person cannot ride a bike. Bikes like the Mongoose Dolomite, the Zize Bikes 29Er Max, and the Firestrong Bruiser Cruiser are all designed with 400-pound riders in mind.

What is the weight limit for a 26-inch mountain bike?

A 26-inch mountain bike can accommodate up to 250 pounds. This limit applies to all 26-inch bikes, not just mountain bikes.

As with bikes for adults or larger people, you’ll want to take into account what kind of stress you’ll be putting the bike through, what kind of frame the bike has, and whether you want to invest in sturdier wheels when deciding what kind of 26-inch mountain bike to buy.

Final Thoughts 

The short answer to “how much weight can my mountain bike hold?” is “300 pounds.” If you’re a heavier rider and you’re planning to do lots of intense riding, you may want to invest in a steel frame and some stronger wheels.

If you’re a casual rider who just wants to do some low-intensity riding on a bike with a comfortable frame and feel, an aluminum bike like the 3700 may be right for you.

items.title: 5 Things To Know About A German Shepherd’s Awkward Stage
items.summary: German Shepards are noble creatures who live to work and have a lot of energy to burn. Add in the…
items.content_html:
German Shepards are noble creatures who live to work and have a lot of energy to burn. Add in the playfulness of a puppy and you've got a lot of work on your hands and a best friend for life. 

At what age does a German Shepherd calm down?

"Calm" is a relative term for this breed, as they are built to have a lot of energy and require plenty of exercise. Keeping your German Shepherd mentally and physically engaged will create more calm as the dog will feel like it has completed its work for the day. 

By the age of 2, the German Shepherd should be out of the puppy and adolescent stage and be calmer than it was before, but never expect this dog to be a couch potato. It is always going to want to work for you and with you.,

Training your German Shepherd from the first day is key to building on the strengths of this breed and eliminating any bad behavior. 

A big part of your dog being calm is if you are calm, according to dog expert Cesar Milan.

At what age do German shepherds show hip dysplasia?

Large breed dogs like German Shepherds can show signs of hip dysplasia as early as four months old, but it can manifest at any age. 

Start with your first vet visit to test the mobility and flexibility of the hips. Then, as your German Shepherd ages, look for hallmark signs of hip issues:

  • Reluctance or difficulty around stairs, or during running and jumping
  • Lameness in the back legs
  • "Bunny Hopping" gait
  • Decreased activity (especially in such an active breed like a German Shepherd

Another big health concern for aging German Shepherds is Degenerative Myelopathy. This is a progressive disease in the back limbs that can cause weakness and be paralyzing. 

How long does German Shepherd puppy stage last?

The puppy stage is technically done at six months of age, but that doesn't mean you've got a well-behaved, grown-up dog on your hands. At the six-month mark, the puppy goes onto the Adolescent staff, not reaching full adulthood until 2-3 years. 

It is imperative during the puppy stage you do daily training, around 15 minutes a day, to stop bad behaviors and reinforce good behaviors. Socialization is important as well as taming any bad habits like jumping on people, lunging, or barking ferociously. 

This is also a time to determine if you are going to have the dog spayed or neutered, as both impact the behavior of the dog and how you should handle it going into the next stage. 

What is the hardest puppy stage?

Sometimes the hardest puppy stage depends on your frustration limit. The cute little puppy suddenly starts chewing on shoes or unstuffing a pillow, and if you are too busy taking "cute" photos of the dog instead of correcting the behavior, you are going to find many hard stages. Potty training can make the best dog owner feel overwhelmed if not done properly with crate training and reinforcing correct potty moments. 

Ask any German Shepherd owner and they'll probably tell you the hardest puppy stage isn't a puppy stage at all. It's the adolescent stage. The dogs have been trained enough to know better, but are now big and strong enough to barrel through their energy with the zest of a teenager and are more than willing to test their boundaries. 

Sadly, this is the stage when many owners give up and take the dogs to the shelter, deeming them "difficult" or "aggressive", when it's really just the owner's lack of knowledge or willingness to work with this brilliant and teachable breed. 

Final Thoughts

Make sure you have the time and patience to train a dog with this much energy and dominance. Do deep research on this breed before bringing one home. You have a lot of work ahead of you, but it's well worth it. Train the German Shepherd from the earliest stage possible for basic commands like sit, stay, walk on a loose leash, and voice recall. These dogs stay very active throughout their lives and you'll need to keep up with that energy.

Due to shedding, you'll need to brush the dog every few days, and more during the two times a year when the dogs "blow their coat."

Consider training your German Shepherd to the standard of the AKC Good Canine Citizen program and you'll have a friendly, approachable dog who will also defend your family in times of crisis.

items.title: About
items.summary: I grew up in the Texas Hill Country and love it out here. Not only that, but I've garnered a…
items.content_html:
About NATUREOFTEN

A Native Texan living in the hill country.

I grew up in the Texas Hill Country and love it out here. Not only that, but I've garnered a love for all things nature. Follow me on my journey and don't hesitate to reach out if you want me to cover something you're interested in!

Subscribe
